speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't talk about his kids at all. I know. But then now he can introduce them.

speaker: interviewer
text: He does have two. Are you going to have the wife in here also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, everybody will be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to switch to the other chair or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, why don't you Introduce your family to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, you know, you know my wife, Lola. And this is my daughter, Rochelle Fried. And this is my, of course, I'll since-- this is my son-in-law, Ken, Ken Fried, Kenny. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kenny. Right. And this is my, my son, Howard, Howard Lory Kohn. And this is my, my grandson, which is Benjamin Maxwell Fried. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he named after someone special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he is named after-- of course, his name, in Jewish he is named after three grandfathers. Or actually, they're all great grandfathers. It's Benjamin is after my mother-in-law's father. And Gedale, which is after Lola's father. And, and Moishe is after Kenny's grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so we gave him the name Benjamin. And, of course, you know, the last name Moishe, is after his grandfather, which is Maxwell. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I wanted to ask both of the kids what it was like for you growing up with a father who had been through the Holocaust. Do you want to start?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was very difficult at different times in her life. There was, I don't know. There was always a spot inside of my father that we knew we couldn't quite touch or quite reach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know that spot? Can you name it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I can. It's more of a feeling, you know, a distant place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the sense of being a survivor touched both my brother and I very much, in terms of how we both felt growing up. And even in our adult lives, feeling that we have some of that inside of us, that we too are survivors. And so being children of a survivor, I think was a very strong legacy that we received. Both in a good and a difficult sense, cause there was a burden that went with it, in terms of wanting to provide the family that my father had lost, and that we were his family. So there was a lot of burden on--in that sense. And, and then also an incredible strength, that we too could survive whatever life had to deal. Shh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does your son get along with his grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son took one look at his grandfather and decided there was no other person in this world. [LAUGHTER] And when my father is in his presence, none of us exist. They seem to have an incredible bond, just a real special relationship. And I think they're one of the same spirit. So it's like Benjamin represents the, the first step in the renewing of the generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's doing a good job. He has a lot to say. What about you, Howard? What did you get out of your father's experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think from a very young age I always knew that we were different in some way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was always a, well, that feeling that somehow as a family, we were different. There was a pain and joy with that. The painful side is that we were-- we didn't have a lot of relatives that we could have had. There was that void that Rochelle and I had to fill, that my father had no other living relatives, for the most part. And that was hard for us. For me personally, looking at other people, knowing that I was a first generation American, and my heritage was in Europe, and it was all wiped out. And that was a hard-- that was very hard for me to accept. And it took many years for me to accept that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to run away? Do you-- do you have any feelings about just casting that off and not being Jewish and not having Jewish children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes. I think more so me than Rochelle. That is probably exactly how I feel because of that. I didn't like being different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt very-- the need to definitely assimilate, wanting to forget the past. Because all the time that I was growing up in Hebrew school, they always-- made to never forget. It was in my face 24 hours a day, especially since my father was directly involved. I couldn't just-- I couldn't come home and forget about what I learned about in Hebrew school or what films that I saw about the Holocaust. I was living it every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was very hard to accept.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was opposite. I reacted completely in an opposite way. For me, it deepened the need to have that Jewish identity and to wear it almost like a badge, never to be ashamed that I was Jewish, to want my family to-- my son to have a Jewish heritage and to let the world know that I was Jewish and never want to hide that. So we had very opposite reactions in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it changing with time, Howard? Or do you still feel uncomfortable with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a-- I just recently came back from Europe. And I had the opportunity to be in Germany-- in two different cities, in Munich and Berlin. And I had an experience one day, walking through a mall, at about rush hour, when there was thousands of people in the square. And I got very nervous. I felt all of a sudden what my father may have felt being in Germany, being a lone person with enemies all around you. And it was very scary. I got very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't think it has passed. I think I'll always have those feelings inside me. And I'm just learning to deal with it. Thank you. What about you coming into this family? Did you have any Holocaust survivors in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't directly in my own family. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'd have to say that it's really been you know, a learning experience for me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, through my wife and her relationships with her family, I can begin to understand [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] what, what the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what the experience [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the reality of, of the experience, might have been for Martin and then how the woman in his life, his wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adapted to that reality and began to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] take on some of that herself. And the pain, the, the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the part of it that doesn't make any sense, that's mind boggling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The part of wanting to help survivors to continue to survive. And I see that quality in, in, in Lola. She's very life giving and life supporting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And with, with my own wife, she has a lot of those qualities too. As she said, she's very-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm not sure proud is the right word. She's, she's sure of herself in the world, that she has a right to be in the world, and that her family has a right to be in the world, as Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so she has a sense of commitment to that, a sense of confidence around that. There is a downside to it or a painful side. I don't know if the two words are synonymous, but-- and the painful side, I think, through [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my experience with my wife is the burden, the legacy that she holds for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through her father. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think there's just a certain part of, of, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of what this legacy has been for, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for her that has been-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that, that-- I think there's an aspect of my wife that's fragile at the same time. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think that it gave her a certain sense of what's possible, in terms of, of horror and the inhumanity that's possible. And I think it, it gave her an underlying, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't want to say fear but kind of-- yeah, a level of fear that is not very apparent, but it's there. And it's a fear about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how much to really kind of go all out in life, in terms of self-expression. I think it's around self-expression [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and where she-- and how she feels freedom to take new risks and who she is and how the world might be for her. And I'm not sure if that makes sense. But it's kind of hard for me to really, again, to articulate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's only been one generation, right? She's, she understood, she was willing to understand what happened. I don't want to analyze here. I just thank you very much. That was a very sensitive portrait. I wanted to ask you, Martin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --What you have to say to-- what is important for you to say to your great grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the importance to my great grandchildren, to the next generations, that they should really, regardless, I mean what the-- and I mean in other words, what life might bring. But you know I would like them to really associate themselves you know-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] what-- with our-- you know, I mean, of course, this is their country. But I mean to remember you know, that Israel is really our homeland, to a point. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, loyalty could be to the country that you live in, it's your loyalty. But I mean, Jews, for so many generations, going through [SOBS] what they went through. Still, in all their roads, looking back to our-- [SOBBING] to our homeland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I think that the, the burden for survivors to carry, the burden that they have, is really Israel. Becoming a country made it much easier to carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Martin.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you all of you for being here. We're going to see the films now, photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right is my father. He is approximately in his 30's, about 35 years old. His name is Lipot Kohn. And it was taken in Satu Mare, Romania. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was for, for, you know, for identification purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the '30s. And somebody, a neighbor, found it after the war and gave it to me. And to the right is my mother, when she was about 17. She was still single. And her name is Rivkah, Rivkah Kohn. I mean, rather, I beg your pardon, Rivkah Braun was her maiden name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sorry, she's on the left. This picture is, is a picture of my mother's family. And there's five sisters in that particular picture. To the right was her younger sister, her name was Jittu, Jittu Braun. And next to her is Chaya, my tante Chaya. And-- Chaya Braun. And next to her is Esther, Esther Braun. And then the fourth one is my mother. And next to her is her sister, Ruchy. And then in the middle of the picture, with the baby, is Sarina, her name is Sarina. And she was sort of like a nanny, I think, and also a friend of the family. And the baby over there is a-- my eldest cousin from the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone survive in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Sarina survived the war. How, I don't know. But you know, in the middle of the picture. And of course Nachmen, you know, they went in 1944 or the end of 43, they went to Israel. So he survived. But, of course, since then, he passed on.

speaker: interviewer
text: His sisters were all killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not all. No, I beg your pardon, there's another aunt. Second to my right, which is Chaya, she survived. And she was taken to a camp near Vienna, which was supposed to be like a-- the Germans showed it off, you know, that how they are treating the prisoners. And she, she survived over there. And then of course, later on, they moved to Israel. Well, I'm going to start from the left with my father. And his name was Lipot Kohn. And right behind him is my brother, Nachmen, who was the eldest. And then right next to him, behind, is, is me, myself, Martin, Martin Kohn. And then I'm going to start from the extreme right with my sister, you know, the sister next to me. Her name was Eva, Eva Kohn. And next to her was my sister Roza, Roza Kohn. And then, of course, in the middle there is my mother holding the baby, his name was Moishe. And then right next to him, my father holding the baby, he was Jossi, Joseph Chaim Kohn. And this particular picture, my aunt, my father sent this particular picture. It was also a passport picture. And my father made an extra picture and sent it to his sister in Israel, who lived at that time in Mea Shearim. And she send it to me after the war. [SOBS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have be taken, I don't think I was more than [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seven or eight, you know. It must have been around in 19-- no, I think this was in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is anyone missing out of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my sister, my little sister, who was five years old. She was born only in '40, so that she is not there. And of course the other picture, which you had before, an aunt of mine, Ruchy, who lived in Timisoara, Romania. And she, she gave me that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Satu Mare, in Romania. Like I mentioned, it was a passport picture. This picture was taken in our home town, where we lived in Satu Mare. To the left, that's my sister, Eva, or we called her [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] Kohn. And the middle picture is sort of like a removed cousin, to say, not a cousin. Her name was Hannah Friend. And they-- she survived by going to Israel in 1943, end of '43 or beginning of '44. And next to her is a cousin of hers, who was our neighbor. And her name was Ruchy Tietelbaum. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It was probably taken in 1942 or '43, I would say. Well, this picture was taken right-- just a couple of months after I came to the United States. And at the right, bottom right, it's me Martin, Martin Kohn. And then the other three are my cousins. Right directly behind me is David Braun. And the eldest one is to the left, Nachmen, Nachmen Braun standing. And at the bottom is, we call him Arthur or [NON-ENGLISH] Braun. And also, you know, these two cousins survived, the upper two, survived in Budapest, you know. And they were able to, you know, stay in Budapest. But [NON-ENGLISH], Arthur, was deported. And he was also, you know, right after the war, in 1944, he was in the Warsaw ghetto to dismantle the ghetto, worked over there. And so that's, that's the story in that picture. Well, to the, to the left is my embarkation card, you know, the one which I--I came to the United States, the ship, came to the United States in March. I arrived in New York, March 14, 1947. And the ship was Marine Flasher. It was a liberty ship. And to the right, the picture on the right is a, is a-- I don't really recall. But it must have been some kind of a identification picture that I-- I don't think it was my citizenship paper. But it was sort of maybe a green card or some kind of-- this was taken, though, in the United States, when I first probably came.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was 1947. Or it might have been later, I don't really recall. Well, this picture is our wedding picture of Lola. Lola, of course then she was Lola Krinsky, and then, of course, Lola Kohn. And to the left is me, Martin, Martin Kohn. And we were married in 1954, October 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, in the Gold Manor on Willoughby, Willoughby Avenue. In Brooklyn, right across the street from where Lola lived, or vis a vis. Yeah, this picture was taken on the our 40th anniversary celebration. To the right of-- you know, the picture, is me, Martin Kohn. And then right next to me, holding the baby, Benjamin Fried, is my son, Howard Kohn. And next to Howard is my wife, my lovely wife, Lola, and my lovely daughter, Rochelle. And of course my son-in-law, Kenny Fried.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken some time in October, October, in a hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the family of my wife's mother's parents. And from the left is Benjamin, Benjamin Mohler. And next, standing, is his wife, Hannah, Hannah Mohler. And next to her is Leigo, Leigo Mohler, who was their son, my mother-in-law's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it take and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was taken in Krynica, Poland, approximately in the '20s. Exactly when, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they all perished in, in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from my wife's father's side. And from the left is her grandmother, Babche, Babche Krinsky. And to the right is her grandfather, Tzalo Krinsky. And by the way, these people were actually not even sent to a camp. But they were murdered right in their hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken also in Krynica, in Krynica, Poland. When the picture was taken, I don't know. But it probably must have taken or in the '20s or early '30s. This is a picture of my wife's parents. And to the left is my mother-in-law, Anya, Anya Krinsky. And to the right is my father-in-law, Gedale, Gedale Krinsky. And it was taken in 1947, in, in Brooklyn, New York.
